name: Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail
name_original: Ubrique - Benaocaz – circular por la Calzada Romana
description: Hike the moderate 5.9-mile Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail in Sierra de Grazalema, a historic journey taking about 3 hours and 15 minut
region_intro: Hiking around Ubrique offers access to diverse landscapes situated between the Sierra de Grazalema and Los Alcornocales Natural Parks. The region features rocky formations, deep canyons, and extensive cork oak forests. Surrounded by mountain ranges like the Sierras de Libar and Ubrique, the area is crisscrossed by rivers and waterways, providing varied terrain for outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around Ubrique The most popular hiking route is Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail, a 5.9 miles (9.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 15 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows a well-preserved Roman road through an oak forest. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ocuri Archaeological Site – Casa del Capitán, Puerto Tirao loop from Ubrique, a moderate 6.5 miles (10.4 km) path. This trail passes by the Ocuri Archaeological Site, offering historical insights and views of the surrounding mountains. Local hikers also love the Casa del Capitán, Puerto Tirao – Ubrique loop from Ubrique, a 4.1 miles (6.6 km) trail leading through rolling hills and offering views of Ubrique, often completed in about 1 hour 54 minutes. Hiking around Ubrique is defined by rocky landscapes, cork oak forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy strolls, moderate excursions, and challenging ascents. The routes in Ubrique are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 500 hikers have used komoot to explore Ubrique's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Ubrique?
A: There are over 20 hiking routes around Ubrique, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include 7 easy, 15 moderate, and 3 difficult trails, ensuring options for various fitness levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking near Ubrique?
A: Ubrique is uniquely situated between the Sierra de Grazalema and Los Alcornocales Natural Parks. You'll encounter dramatic rocky formations, deep canyons, and extensive cork oak forests. The region is also crisscrossed by rivers and surrounded by mountain ranges like the Sierras de Libar and Ubrique, offering varied terrain and stunning panoramic views.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Ubrique?
A: Yes, Ubrique offers several easy trails suitable for families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31480922/casa-del-capitan-puerto-tirao-ubrique-loop-from-ubrique" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa del Capitán, Puerto Tirao – Ubrique loop from Ubrique&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 6.6 km path that takes less than 2 hours, leading through rolling hills with views of the town.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes around Ubrique?
A: Many trails around Ubrique are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31484648/ocuri-archaeological-site-casa-del-capitan-puerto-tirao-loop-from-ubrique" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ocuri Archaeological Site – Casa del Capitán, Puerto Tirao loop from Ubrique&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 10.4 km route that offers historical insights and scenic views.

Q: What historical or natural attractions can I explore on the trails?
A: Hiking in Ubrique allows you to discover significant historical and natural sites. You can walk along the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995976938/ubrique-to-benaocaz-roman-road-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which passes through an oak forest. Another route leads to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31484648/ocuri-archaeological-site-casa-del-capitan-puerto-tirao-loop-from-ubrique" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ocuri Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;, featuring Roman ruins. Additionally, you can find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5126765" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora&lt;/a&gt; mountain pass or various peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3938203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Torreón Peak&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best season for hiking in Ubrique?
A: Ubrique's climate makes it suitable for hiking for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures. While the Sierra de Grazalema is known for being the rainiest spot on the Iberian Peninsula, this contributes to lush landscapes. Winter hiking is also possible, but it's advisable to check local weather conditions, especially for higher elevations.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for those seeking a challenge, Ubrique offers difficult trails. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14589550/navazo-alto-villaluenga-del-rosario-loop-from-ubrique" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navazo Alto – Villaluenga del Rosario loop from Ubrique&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 18.3 km route with significant elevation changes, typically taking over 6 hours to complete.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Ubrique?
A: The hiking routes in Ubrique are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.4 out of 5 stars from over 100 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from ancient Roman roads through oak forests to trails offering panoramic views of the surrounding natural parks.

Q: Are there opportunities for wild swimming or water features along the trails?
A: Ubrique is crisscrossed by rivers and waterways, including the Ubrique River, which flows into the Los Hurones Dam. While specific wild swimming spots on trails are not detailed, the presence of these water bodies suggests opportunities for enjoying natural water features, especially along routes like the River Ubrique Route.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads in Ubrique?
A: As a popular destination for outdoor activities, Ubrique generally offers parking options within the town, from which many trails begin or are easily accessible. It's recommended to check specific trailhead information or local signage for the most convenient parking.

Q: What is the typical duration for hikes around Ubrique?
A: Hiking durations vary significantly depending on the trail's length and difficulty. Easy routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31480922/casa-del-capitan-puerto-tirao-ubrique-loop-from-ubrique" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa del Capitán, Puerto Tirao – Ubrique loop&lt;/a&gt;, can be completed in under 2 hours. Moderate trails, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995976938/ubrique-to-benaocaz-roman-road-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail&lt;/a&gt;, typically take around 3 hours and 15 minutes, while difficult routes can extend to over 6 hours.
tips: A short and easy walk along an ancient Roman road from the 1st century BC. restored. It connects Benaocaz and Ubrique. Due to the paving, it is advisable to wear comfortable shoes. (How the Romans did it back then is a mystery to me 😏...).
This trail takes us from Benaocaz to Ubrique along a Roman road. Length: 3.4km

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to hike the Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail, and are there any specific regulations for the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself generally doesn't require a specific permit for access, it is located within the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park. This park has regulations to protect its unique environment, especially concerning fire, waste disposal, and sometimes access to certain sensitive areas during specific seasons (e.g., fire risk periods). Always check local park authority guidelines before your visit. There are no entrance fees for the park.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail, and is public transport an option?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in both Ubrique and Benaocaz, the two villages connected by the trail. Specific trailhead parking areas might be available on the outskirts of these towns. For public transport, local bus services connect Ubrique and Benaocaz with other towns in the region, making it possible to hike one-way and use public transport for your return journey.

faqs.question: Is the Ubrique to Benaocaz Roman Road Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally considered dog-friendly. However, as it's within a Natural Park, always keep your dog on a leash to protect wildlife and respect other hikers. Be mindful of farm animals you might encounter, such as goats, mules, or cows, and ensure your dog is under control.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this Roman Road trail?
faqs.answer: The best times to hike are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can be very hot and exposed, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable if you visit then. Winter can be pleasant, but the ancient stone surface can become very slippery when wet, requiring extra caution.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Roman Road trail?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily follows a remarkably well-preserved ancient Roman road, characterized by its stone pavement. This means much of the path consists of uneven historic stones, requiring sturdy footwear and careful footing. Some sections are better preserved than others. The scenery varies from a confined gorge along the Arroyo Seco valley to open grasslands with olive and oak trees, and eventually an oak forest closer to Ubrique. You'll traverse a calcareous landscape with dolomites, clays, and gypsum.

faqs.question: What should I know about the trail's difficulty, especially regarding the ancient stone surface?
faqs.answer: The trail is rated as moderate, requiring good fitness and sure-footedness. A key consideration is the ancient stone surface, which can be rocky and very slippery, especially in wet conditions. If you hike from Ubrique to Benaocaz, it's a continuous ascent of about 400 meters. Conversely, starting in Benaocaz leads to a continuous descent towards Ubrique, which some hikers find easier on the knees but still requires careful footing on the uneven stones. The trail can also be exposed to the sun, so sun protection and hydration are crucial.

faqs.question: What historical and natural sights can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll literally walk on a tangible link to the past – a Roman road with ancient drainage systems, culverts, and bridges. The trail connects the charming 'white villages' of Ubrique and Benaocaz. Along the way, you'll enjoy panoramic views of the Sierra de Grazalema, including Sierra Alta and the Sierra de Ubrique. Keep an eye out for a sub-forest of mastic and broom, aromatic plants like pennyroyal, dwarf palm, and oleander, and potentially farm animals. The route also passes near archaeological sites, such as the Roman town of Ocvri (Ocuri). Don't miss the well-preserved sections of the Roman Road Between Benaocaz and Ubrique.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this Roman Road trail?
faqs.answer: The full circular route, as described, is about 9.4 km and typically takes around 3 hours and 15 minutes to complete. However, the duration can vary depending on your pace, the number of stops you make for photos or rest, and whether you choose a shorter one-way section. Allow extra time to explore the villages of Ubrique or Benaocaz.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the uneven and potentially slippery stone surface, sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support and grip are essential. Bring plenty of water, especially on sunny days, as the trail can be exposed. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is also highly recommended. Depending on the season, layers of clothing are advisable. Trekking poles can be helpful for stability on the rocky sections.

faqs.question: Is the trail a circular route, or is it typically hiked one-way?
faqs.answer: The trail can be hiked as a circular route, as detailed in this tour, which is approximately 9.4 km. However, many hikers also choose to do it as a one-way hike between Ubrique and Benaocaz, using public transport or arranging a pick-up at the other end. The one-way distance is typically between 3.4 km and 6.1 km, depending on the exact starting and ending points within the villages.

faqs.question: Does the trail connect with other hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other paths. Notably, it follows a significant portion of the Andalucía (Province of Cádiz) trail and is known locally as LA CALZADA ROMANA. You'll also find it intersects with parts of the Sendero Calzada Romana and the Calzada medieval Ubrique - Benaocaz.
